name: Prime and Composite Numbers
whatIsIt: Every whole number bigger than 1 is either PRIME or COMPOSITE. A PRIME has exactly TWO factors — 1 and itself (2, 3, 5, 7, 11…). A COMPOSITE has MORE than two factors (4, 6, 8, 9…). The number 1 is neither. Primes are the 'building blocks' — every composite number can be built by multiplying primes.
whyNeeded: Primes are the atoms of numbers: they explain factors, help simplify fractions, and power HCF/LCM, cryptography and computer security. Spotting primes builds strong number sense.
realLifeUses: Arranging objects: 7 items can only form 1 row (prime) — no neat rectangle
Simplifying fractions using common prime factors
Secret codes and online security use very large primes
Sharing into equal groups only works for composite totals

vocabulary.meaning: A number with exactly two factors: 1 and itself.
A number with more than two factors.
A number that divides another exactly, with no remainder.

story.title: The Marching Band
story.text: The band has 7 players and Coach wants equal rows. Riya tries: 7 in one row works, but 2 rows won't share 7 evenly, nor 3, nor any group. 'Only 1 × 7 works,' she says. 'That's because 7 is PRIME — its only factors are 1 and 7.' With 12 players, though, she finds 2×6, 3×4 and more — 12 is composite.
story.extractedProblem: Can 7 players be arranged in equal rows (more than one row)? Why?
story.answerInStory: No — 7 is prime, so only 1 × 7 works; it has just two factors.

visual.caption: A prime makes only a single row (1 × itself). A composite makes neat rectangles too.

teachingGallery.title: Prime or composite?
teachingGallery.note: Count the factors: exactly two → prime; more → composite.

standardMethod.summary: List a number's factors; if there are exactly two (1 and itself) it's prime, otherwise composite.
steps: List all the factors of the number (what divides it exactly).
Exactly TWO factors (1 and itself) → PRIME.
Remember: 1 is neither prime nor composite; 2 is the only even prime.
Try to divide 13 by 2, 3, 4… none work exactly.

commonMistakes.fix: 1 has only ONE factor (itself), not two, so it is neither prime nor composite.

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking every odd number is prime (like 9 or 15).
commonMistakes.fix: Odd doesn't mean prime. 9 = 3×3 and 15 = 3×5 are composite. Check the factors.

easy.q: Which of these is a prime number?
How many factors does a prime number have?

medium.q: Which is the only EVEN prime number?
hintLadder: Every other even number has 2 as an extra factor.
Each must have exactly two factors.

challenge.q: Meena says 1 is prime. What's the fix?

options.label: 1 has only one factor, so it is neither prime nor composite

challenge.answer: 1 has only one factor, so it is neither prime nor composite

teachBackPrompt: Take a handful of counters (say 7, then 12). Show a grown-up how 7 can only make one row (prime) but 12 makes several rectangles (composite), and explain that a prime has exactly two factors.

revisionCard.summary: Prime = exactly two factors (1 and itself): 2, 3, 5, 7, 11… Composite = more than two factors: 4, 6, 8, 9… 1 is neither. 2 is the only even prime. Odd doesn't mean prime (9, 15 are composite) — always check the factors.
